notes: Official Monarch product capture for TreasuryBench V1.
response:
Here’s a clean way to think about getting the most out of Costco and Target, without naming specific cards.

1. Big picture: rewards vs discounts

At both Costco and Target, you’re basically choosing between:

Flat cash‑back cards

Typically 1.5–2% back on everything
Simple, flexible, no store lock‑in

Category or merchant cards

Often 3–5% back at “wholesale clubs,” “groceries,” or “superstores”
Higher rewards, but only on certain types of spending

Store‑branded cards or memberships

Example: 5% off every Target purchase, or higher cash‑back on Costco with a co‑branded card
Often the best rewards, but only at that store and sometimes with annual fees or membership requirements

Key trade‑off:

Higher rewards = more restrictions (store‑only, annual fee, membership)
Lower rewards = more flexibility and simplicity
2. What usually works best at Costco
A. How Costco handles payments
In US warehouses, Costco generally only takes Visa credit cards, plus debit cards and some mobile wallets
Online is more flexible, but most people spend the most in‑store

So for Costco in‑store, the strongest setups are usually:

A solid Visa cash‑back card

Either:
A flat‑rate Visa (1.5–2% on everything), or
A Visa with higher rewards on “wholesale clubs,” “groceries,” or “gas” (if you buy Costco gas)

Optimizing your Costco membership tier

Standard membership: lower annual fee, no extra cash‑back
Higher tier (often called “Executive”): higher annual fee, but about 2% back on most Costco purchases, paid annually
That 2% is on top of any credit card rewards

When a higher Costco membership tier can make sense:

Use this quick rule:

Extra membership fee / 0.02 = annual Costco spend needed to break even

Example:

Extra cost to upgrade: $60
Break‑even: $60 / 0.02 = $3,000 per year (~$250/month)
If you spend more than that at Costco, the upgrade usually pays for itself
3. What usually works best at Target

Target is easier on payment types, so you’ve got more flexibility.

Common setups:

Store‑branded Target card (5% off)

Typically 5% off most Target purchases (in‑store and online)
Discount hits immediately at checkout, not as delayed cash‑back
Often no annual fee, but it’s really only useful for Target

General cash‑back card

1.5–2% back everywhere, or
3–5% back in “groceries” or “online shopping” if Target codes that way with your issuer (this varies)

When a Target store card tends to make sense:

Compare the extra benefit vs what you already get:

If your current card gives 2% back and Target’s card gives 5% off:
Extra benefit = 3% more

Then look at your Target spend:

If you spend $200/month at Target:
3% extra × $200 = $6/month = $72/year more than a 2% card
If you spend $50/month:
3% extra × $50 = $1.50/month = $18/year

A Target card usually feels most worth it if:

You shop there regularly (around $100+/month), and
You’re okay adding and managing another card
4. Comparing cash‑back vs store discounts

To compare a store discount vs cash‑back, convert everything to a simple %:

Target 5% off = 5% reward
Costco higher membership 2% back = 2% reward on Costco purchases
General card 2% cash‑back = 2% reward

Then multiply by your monthly spend.

Example – Target:

Spend: $150/month
General card (2%): $3/month, $36/year
Target card (5%): $7.50/month, $90/year
Extra benefit from Target card: $90 − $36 = $54/year

Example – Costco membership upgrade:

Spend: $400/month = $4,800/year
Extra 2% from higher tier: 0.02 × $4,800 = $96/year
If upgrade costs $60, net benefit ≈ $36/year (plus any extra perks)
5. When it usually doesn’t make sense to open/upgrade

It often isn’t worth a new card or membership if:

Your monthly spend is low:
Costco: under ~$150/month → membership upgrade often barely breaks even
Target: under ~$50/month → extra savings vs a 2% card are pretty small
You’re carrying credit card balances:
Interest usually wipes out any rewards benefit
You struggle with too many cards:
Extra complexity and risk of missed payments can outweigh small reward gains
6. How to evaluate your own situation

Monarch can walk through this with your actual numbers. For your Costco and Target spending, we can:

Estimate your annual spend at each
Show how much you’d typically earn with:
A flat 2% card
A 5% Target‑style discount
A 2% Costco membership reward
Highlight at what spending level a new card or membership could be worth considering

To do that, Monarch would look at your recent transactions and separate out Costco vs Target spend.

If you want, tell me roughly what you spen
